id: Autolycus, blessed thief favored by Hermes
name: Autolycus, blessed thief favored by Hermes
shortDescription: Autolycus was a legendary trickster and master thief in Greek mythology, known for his ability to change the appearance of stolen goods and his divine favor from Hermes.
description: Autolycus was a renowned figure in Greek mythology, primarily recognized as a master thief and trickster. He was the son of Hermes, the messenger god known for his cunning and thievery, and Chione, daughter of Daedalion. Autolycus inherited his father's skills and was considered the most skilled thief of his time, capable not only of stealing but also of changing the appearance of his loot, making it nearly impossible to trace. He was also the maternal grandfather of Odysseus, and played a significant role in his early life. Autolycus named Odysseus, giving him the name that meant 'son of pain' or 'victim of enmity,' reflecting the troubles the child would face. His association with Odysseus highlights the importance of cunning and resourcefulness in Greek heroic narratives. While not a major god or hero, Autolycus represents a darker, more ambiguous side of the heroic ideal, valuing cleverness and deception alongside traditional virtues. His patronage by Hermes emphasizes the god's association with not only commerce and travel but also trickery and the liminal spaces between right and wrong.
